critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing leakage via full-dataset fitting: all preprocessing transformers, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics are computed using the entire 24-month dataset before splitting. For a temporal split, this means the training features encode distributional properties of the test period (months 19–24). Demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) computed across 24 months will be influenced by the second-year patterns that the RL agent is being evaluated on. This is especially problematic for the route efficiency ratio metric: if the "historical median delivery time" used as the baseline is computed on the full dataset, it incorporates test-period delivery times into the denominator of the primary metric itself.

2. The route efficiency ratio is defined as actual_delivery_time / baseline_expected_time, where the baseline is "historical median delivery time for that route segment, stratified by day-of-week, season, and demand level." If this historical baseline is computed from the training set only, it reflects Year 1 performance norms; if computed from the full dataset, it reflects both years. The design does not specify which. If the RL agent improves delivery times in Year 2, the efficiency ratio denominator shifts, making the RL agent's ratio appear closer to 1 even as absolute times decrease. The metric is unstable under the hypothesis it is testing.

3. The DRL agent uses PPO, which is an on-policy algorithm. Applying it to a historical dataset via "50 epochs with experience replay" treats historical transitions as if they were generated by the current policy. This is the standard offline RL setting, but PPO is not designed for it — the importance ratio clipping in PPO assumes that the behavior policy and current policy are similar (controlled by the clip ratio), which is unlikely when the agent is learning from a fixed historical log generated by a different routing policy. The policy gradient estimates will be biased, and the extent of this bias is not characterized.

4. The pre-trained sentiment model applied to customer reviews is used without retraining on this task, but customer satisfaction in logistics routing has a specific vocabulary and expression style that may differ substantially from the model's pre-training domain. Sentiment polarity may not align with the operational construct (on-time delivery satisfaction) and may conflate delivery-time complaints with unrelated service complaints. The design treats this as a neutral pass-through feature but never validates that the sentiment scores are discriminative for the routing quality construct.

5. The comparison between a grid-searched RL agent (learning rate, reward weights, network architecture) and a nearest-neighbor heuristic with 2-opt that receives "equivalent tuning effort" (swap iteration limit, forecast method) is structurally asymmetric. The RL agent has a continuous high-dimensional parameter space; the heuristic has at most a handful of discrete parameters. "Equivalent tuning effort" is not meaningful when the search spaces differ by orders of magnitude. The heuristic likely reaches near-optimal parameter settings quickly, while the RL agent's grid may still be far from its optimum.
all_issues_raised: Full-dataset preprocessing and demand aggregation statistics before temporal split encodes test-period patterns into training features
Route efficiency ratio denominator is ambiguously defined and may shift as the RL agent improves, making the metric unstable under the hypothesis
PPO applied to a fixed historical log with experience replay violates the on-policy assumption and biases policy gradient estimates
Pre-trained sentiment model applied without domain validation, creating an unvalidated feature that may conflate unrelated complaints with routing quality
Tuning effort cannot be equated between an RL agent with a high-dimensional parameter space and a heuristic with a handful of discrete parameters
All preprocessing statistics and scaling parameters fit on full 24-month dataset before temporal splitting, leaking test-period traffic and demand statistics into training normalization

Reward function hyperparameter search optimizes the same components as the primary evaluation metric, creating optimization-evaluation circularity that mechanically improves the metric for the RL agent
Heuristic baseline is tuned on validation data but is described as the current operational standard; tuned baseline does not measure production value, untuned does not measure methodology
Efficiency ratio normalization uses historical median from full dataset; if test period has structural differences in traffic patterns, the normalization baseline is inappropriate
PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical dataset without importance sampling corrections, producing unreliable policy estimates under behavior-policy distribution mismatch
Preprocessing statistics computed on full 24-month dataset before temporal split — test-period demand distribution leaks into training features
PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical replay buffer — architecture mismatch produces incorrect gradient estimates
Efficiency ratio baseline (historical median) computed from full dataset may include test-period delivery times — circularly defined metric
Nearest-neighbor with 2-opt may not represent the actual production baseline — comparison against a potentially weak heuristic
Reward function weights are tuned on validation set — reward specification is a hyperparameter selected using the evaluation distribution
